edges.description: Intercalated-disc disruption redistributes gap-junction proteins and reduces effective electrical coupling between cardiomyocytes.
Desmosomal disruption also alters sodium-channel localization and slows ventricular conduction.
Electrical uncoupling contributes to ventricular tachycardia as a major clinical arrhythmic outcome.
Malignant ventricular arrhythmia can cause transient cerebral hypoperfusion and syncope.
Slow and heterogeneous conduction supports ventricular tachycardia.
Ventricular conduction and repolarization abnormalities manifest on ECG as right-precordial T-wave inversion.
Severe ventricular arrhythmia can degenerate into sudden cardiac death.
Progressive structural dysfunction leads to right ventricular dilatation.
Advanced ventricular dysfunction progresses to symptomatic heart failure.

nodes.description: Antiarrhythmic drugs are used for symptomatic arrhythmia control.

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: diagnosis, genetic background, and risk management.
evidence.snippet: Since ventricular arrhythmias and cardiac arrest occur frequently during or after physical exercise or may be triggered by catecholamines, non-class III antiadrenergic beta-blockers are recommended. In the absence of adequate antiarrhythmic response, sotalol in an appropriate dose is the drug of first choice. Alternatively, amiodarone and flecainide have been reported to be useful

nodes.description: Catheter ablation is used for recurrent ventricular tachycardia in selected patients.

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: diagnosis, genetic background, and risk management.
evidence.snippet: Catheter ablation is an alternative in patients who are refractory to drug treatment and have frequent VT episodes (with a predominantly single morphology)

nodes.description: Later disease stages can progress to clinically significant heart failure.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenic variants in plakophilin-2 gene (PKP2) are associated with better survival in arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: Patients carrying a PKP2 mutation were younger at diagnosis (p = 0.003), more often had negative T waves in V1-V3 (p = 0.01), had higher left ventricular ejection fraction (p = 0.04), and were less likely to present symptoms of heart failure (p = 0.01)
DSG2 | HGNC:3049 | arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy | MONDO:0016587 | AD | Definitive

nodes.description: The dominant proximal mechanism in ARVC is disruption of desmosomal cell-cell adhesion at the cardiomyocyte intercalated disc, most often from pathogenic variants in PKP2, DSP, DSG2, DSC2, or JUP.

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: diagnosis, genetic background, and risk management.
evidence.snippet: The molecular genetic substrate for the disease is mainly acknowledged in genes encoding desmosomal adhesion proteins in the intercalated disk.

nodes.description: Progressive loss of cardiomyocytes with fibrofatty replacement produces structural ventricular disease and later-stage ventricular dysfunction. This is the ARVC-specific form of adverse ventricular remodeling, in which desmosomal cardiomyocyte loss is replaced by fibrous and adipose tissue rather than the interstitial collagen fibrosis of dilated/hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: diagnosis, genetic background, and risk management.
evidence.snippet: Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy (AC), also known as arrhythmogenic right ventricular dysplasia/cardiomyopathy (ARVD/C), is a hereditary disease characterised by ventricular arrhythmias, right ventricular and/or left ventricular dysfunction, and fibrofatty replacement of cardiomyocytes.

nodes.description: Altered connexin43 distribution reduces effective electrical coupling between ventricular cardiomyocytes.

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: diagnosis, genetic background, and risk management.
evidence.snippet: These alterations give rise to electrical cell-cell uncoupling and slow conduction, respectively, thereby providing a substrate for early activation delay resulting in ventricular tachyarrhythmia, a hallmark of AC

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenic variants in plakophilin-2 gene (PKP2) are associated with better survival in arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: Ten variants (5 frameshift, 2 nonsense, 2 splicing, and 1 missense) in PKP2 were found in 28 (50%) cases.
PKP2 | HGNC:9024 | arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy | MONDO:0016587 | AD | Definitive

nodes.description: Progressive structural disease produces right ventricular enlargement and wall-motion abnormalities.

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: diagnosis, genetic background, and risk management.
evidence.snippet: Regional RV akinesia, dyskinesia, or aneurysm  ○ And 1 of the following (end diastole): PLAX RVOT ≥32 mm

nodes.description: Altered sodium-channel localization slows conduction in ventricular myocardium and enlarges the arrhythmogenic substrate.

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: diagnosis, genetic background, and risk management.
evidence.snippet: These alterations give rise to electrical cell-cell uncoupling and slow conduction, respectively, thereby providing a substrate for early activation delay resulting in ventricular tachyarrhythmia, a hallmark of AC

nodes.description: Sudden death may be the first manifestation, including in the concealed stage.

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: diagnosis, genetic background, and risk management.
evidence.snippet: However, sudden cardiac death (SCD) may be the first manifestation, often at young age in the concealed stage of disease.

nodes.description: Syncope commonly reflects transient cerebral hypoperfusion from malignant ventricular arrhythmia.

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: diagnosis, genetic background, and risk management.
evidence.snippet: Patients with AC typically present between the second and the fourth decade of life with palpitations, lightheadedness, or syncope due to ventricular ectopy or (monomorphic) ventricular tachycardia (VT) with left bundle branch block (LBBB) morphology

nodes.description: T-wave inversion in right precordial leads is part of the diagnostic ECG phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Pathogenic variants in plakophilin-2 gene (PKP2) are associated with better survival in arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy.
evidence.snippet: Patients carrying a PKP2 mutation were younger at diagnosis (p = 0.003), more often had negative T waves in V1-V3 (p = 0.01)

nodes.description: Monomorphic ventricular tachycardia with left bundle branch block morphology is a classic presentation.

evidence.reference_title: Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: diagnosis, genetic background, and risk management.
evidence.snippet: Patients with AC typically present between the second and the fourth decade of life with palpitations, lightheadedness, or syncope due to ventricular ectopy or (monomorphic) ventricular tachycardia (VT) with left bundle branch block (LBBB) morphology
